title: Wandering Galaxy - A Masterpiece of Science Fiction Adventure Gaming
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=smATVd6dk-k

Daniel (@dungeondive) is absolutely smitten with Wandering Galaxy, the newest Crossroads game from Plaid Hat Games by designer Jerry Hawthorne. This blue-collar space adventure drops you and your oddball crew aboard the Zephyr—a decrepit ship piloted by a senile AI—tasked with scraping together enough profit to pay off a rather unsavory debt collector. The four-chapter campaign format (only four sessions each!) keeps the experience snappy enough to actually *finish*, while the browser-based encounter system serves up hundreds of complex scenarios without requiring downloads. Daniel's barely holding back his enthusiasm, candidly admitting he'd likely declare this his game of the year despite it being February.

What makes Wandering Galaxy sing is its thoughtful systems integration. Every component interlocks beautifully—from the six ship stations you'll staff with sidekicks and skills, to the modular location guide that opens like Russian nesting dolls as you explore. Combat eschews traditional minis-moving in favor of directional weapon angles and enemy blips circling your stationary vessel, creating tactical puzzles that actually feel meaningful. Between the job deck's sheer variety, ship upgrades, faction reputation, cargo deals, and skill customization through deck-building mechanics, the sandbox breathes with possibility and consequence.

Daniel's lone gripe? He wishes the galaxy map were mounted on chipboard instead of thick cardstock. Beyond that minor quibble, this is one of those rare games that rewards careful play and manages to feel like both a contained board game experience and a genuine RPG in cardboard form.

----

What aspect of Wandering Galaxy appeals to you most—the open-ended job variety, the deck-building character progression, or the ingenious web-app integration? Would this scratch your blue-collar space adventure itch?
